Yesterday we celebrated pharmacists.

Today let's talk about what they actually need.

The average community pharmacist works 47 hours a week.
90% say their workload has increased in the last 2 years.
Funding per item has decreased in real terms since 2015.
Yet pharmacy now delivers more clinical services than ever before.

The question isn't whether pharmacists are good enough. It's whether the system supports them enough.

We built Titan because the tools pharmacists were given didn't match the job they were being asked to do.

posts.idea: REFRAMED as the post-event closer for the 2-week push. Big thesis carousel: pharmacy's problem isn't lack of tools, it's lack of an operating system. Ties the TitanUp conversations into the Titanverse category narrative. Function: Positioning.
posts.post_caption:
Most pharmacy owners are trying to run a service-led business on a system built for dispensing volume.

That is not a strategy problem. It is an infrastructure one.

A dispensing PMR is optimised for moving items through a dispensary. That is exactly what it was designed to do. It does it well.

But a service-led pharmacy needs consultation workflows, integrated prescribing, connected payment, automatic follow-up, and governance that happens inside the work rather than after it. A dispensing PMR does not have those things. Not because it is bad software. Because it was built for a different job.

Adding a booking platform, a consultation tool, a prescribing system and a payment portal does not solve the infrastructure problem. It creates five separate tools where one connected system should be.

There is no version of a serious clinical pharmacy operation that runs sustainably on infrastructure that was never designed for it.

#Titanverse #PharmacyServices #ClinicalPharmacy #PharmacyBusiness

posts.idea:
7-slide carousel — The Relationship That Never Happened
Focus: Private care economics and CRM

OPEN LOOP MECHANISM: Slide 1 opens the loop with a specific, recognisable scenario — private patient pays, leaves, never returns. The loop is: why does that keep happening, and what changes it? Titanverse arrives on slide 6 as the infrastructure that closes the gap, not as a product pitch.

---

SLIDE 1 — Hook
"A patient pays £55 for a private weight management consultation."
"They leave."
"You never speak to them again."
Three lines. Each on its own row. Generous space between them. The third line lands as the problem — the reader asks: is that wrong? Swipe to find out.
Background: near-black (#1a1a1a). Anton, white.

SLIDE 2 — Reframe
"That is not a bad outcome."
"That is the default."
Small header: "In most pharmacies, a private consultation ends when the patient walks out."
No follow-up pathway. No recall system. No next appointment.
Background: near-black. This slide reframes the problem as structural, not personal. The reader recognises their own operation.

SLIDE 3 — Contrast
Split layout. Two columns:
Left: "Other clinical settings" — care record, follow-up booked, reason to return.
Right: "Pharmacy" — transaction ends at the counter.
Same patient. Different infrastructure. Different outcome.
Background: near-black. The visual contrast IS the argument.

SLIDE 4 — Why it happens
"That is not a patient care problem."
"It is a systems problem."
The tools most pharmacies use for private services were not built to track the patient beyond the appointment.
Background: near-black. Blame the infrastructure, not the pharmacist.

SLIDE 5 — The value argument (no fabricated stats)
Three parallel statements in sequence:
"A patient who comes back for review is more valuable than a new one."
"A patient who books travel vaccines because someone followed up is more valuable than a new one."
"A patient who hears from you before their annual review is more valuable than a new one."
All three require a system that owns the relationship after the consultation.
Background: near-black shifting to gradient. The rhythm of the three statements builds weight.

SLIDE 6 — Mechanism reveal
"When follow-up, re-booking triggers and long-term care tracking are built into the same platform as the consultation, the relationship does not end at the door."
"It continues because the system continues it."
Background: full Titanverse gradient (#FF65DD to #B752FF). This is the earned reveal.

SLIDE 7 — Takeaway
"Private services are not just about the appointment."
"They are about whether the patient comes back."
"That only happens when something is looking after the relationship."
Background: Titanverse gradient. One thought per line. Restrained. No hard sell.

---

DESIGN RULES:
— Anton for all statement slides
— SF Pro for supporting text
— Max 15 words on image per slide
— Near-black (#1a1a1a) slides 1—5
— Titanverse gradient (#FF65DD to #B752FF) slides 6—7
— Slide 3 split layout is the visual centrepiece — give it room
— No fabricated statistics anywhere
— No Titanverse logo on image
— 1:1 square aspect ratio
posts.post_caption:
A patient pays for a private weight management consultation.
They leave.
You never speak to them again.

That is not a failure. That is the default.

In most pharmacies, the private consultation ends the moment the patient walks out. No follow-up. No recall. No reason built into the system for them to return.

In any other clinical setting, that patient would have a care record, a follow-up booked, and a pathway back to you.

A returning patient costs less to retain than a new one costs to acquire. Every private service you offer is a potential long-term clinical relationship. Most pharmacies are leaving that on the table because the tools they use were not built to own what happens after the appointment.

#Titanverse #PrivateHealthcare #PharmacyServices #PharmacyBusiness

posts.idea:
INDUSTRY STANCE CAROUSEL — Week of 11 May, counterweight to meme on Mon 11 May.

Angle: 'The NHS wants pharmacists to prescribe. But wanting it and funding the infrastructure are two different things.'

This is the natural follow-through from the meme. Meme lands the joke. This post lands the argument.

Architecture (6 slides):
Slide 1 — Hook: 'The NHS is producing thousands of independent prescribers. Most of them still cannot prescribe in practice.'
Slide 2 — The gap: trained, motivated, registered. But the dispensary queue eats the morning before a single consultation starts.
Slide 3 — The actual blocker: it is not skills. It is not will. It is workflow. The pharmacist is still the bottleneck in every check.
Slide 4 — What pharmacy needs vs. what it has. Clinical capacity vs. manual process overhead.
Slide 5 — The mechanism: what changes when AI clinical checking absorbs the routine. The pharmacist stops being the funnel.
Slide 6 — Close: 'Training more prescribers is not the answer. Freeing the ones you already have is.'

CTA: Engagement question. 'How many IPs in your pharmacy actually get to prescribe regularly? Drop a number below.'

Update: Check C+D and PJ for any fresh IP or NHS workforce angle in early May and fold in the specific news hook before publishing.

Design: Dark backgrounds, bold white type. No Titan branding until slide 6.
posts.post_caption:
The NHS is producing thousands of independent prescribers.

Most of them still cannot prescribe.

Not because they are unwilling.
Not because the training is wrong.

Because the dispensary queue still eats their morning.

Before a single consultation starts, the pharmacist is already three hours into the check stack.

The NHS 10-year plan wants pharmacy at the centre of clinical care.

But wanting it and funding the infrastructure for it are not the same thing.

The pharmacists are trained.
The will is there.
The workflow is not.

Fix the workflow. Free the prescriber.

👇 How many IPs in your pharmacy actually get to prescribe regularly? Drop a number below.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #IndependentPrescribing #PharmacyWorkflow #NHSPharmacy #PharmacyFirst

Booking: phone call or third-party app.
Risk assessment: paper form.
Prescription: handwritten or typed separately.

TITANVERSE LOOP ANIMATION — Tue 21 Apr. Motion designer asset.

Previous loop (Jan 2026 seamless integrations) showed platform connections. This one shows the SERVICE JOURNEY specifically — a private prescribing consultation from start to finish.

This is differentiated: integrations loop was about what connects TO Titanverse. This loop is about what happens INSIDE a single patient consultation.

Loop concept (8-12 second seamless loop):
— Six stages shown as connected nodes on a curved flow path
— Each stage lights up in sequence: Booking → Pre-consult → Consultation → Prescribing → Payment → Follow-up
— Each node has a small icon (calendar, clipboard, person+pharmacist, Rx, card, bell)
— The path glows as it moves through each stage — then resets and loops
— Subtle sparkle/particle effect on the active node
— Text overlay fades in at the top: 'One consultation. One flow.'

Design:
— Dark purple/near-black Titanverse background
— Titanverse teal/magenta gradient on the flow path
— Clean minimal icons, not illustrations
— Titanverse logo bottom left, sparkle ✨ mark
— No text heavy — the motion tells the story

Pharmacies are being told to do more.

More services. More consultations. More prescribing.

REACTIVE INDUSTRY CAROUSEL — Mon 20 Apr.

FACTUAL RISK FIXED: This is NOT a 'what changed' post. The 2026/27 CPCF was NOT settled. CPE confirmed on 27 March 2026 that negotiations were still ongoing and 2025/26 terms rolled forward unchanged. There are no new confirmed contract changes to announce.

Angle: The contract year has started. The uncertainty has not gone away. Pharmacy owners are running on arrangements that were already inadequate, in a year when costs have increased again. That is the story.

6-slide carousel (revised):
Slide 1 — Hook: 'The new financial year has started. The 2026/27 pharmacy contract has not been agreed.'
Slide 2 — What that means: 2025/26 terms roll forward. Already inadequate last year. Business rates and living wage costs rising from April 2026.
Slide 3 — Pharmacy First cap reality: £17 per consultation, capped at prior year delivery volume. Grew your delivery last year? Your cap reflects that. No rollover of underspent allocations.
Slide 4 — Price concession pressure: below real sourcing cost on significant number of lines. Structural, not temporary.
Slide 5 — The response: not waiting. Getting more clinical output from the same team. Reducing pharmacist time on routine dispensing. Growing services revenue that has no cap.
Slide 6 — Close: 'This is not a blip. The pharmacies absorbing it are building, not waiting.'

Design: Dark. Bold type. Stats as hero elements. No Titan branding until slide 6.
posts.post_caption:
The new financial year started on 1 April.

The 2026/27 pharmacy contract has not been agreed.

Negotiations are still ongoing. Current arrangements are rolling forward.

Which means pharmacies are operating under a settlement that was already inadequate last year, while costs have gone up again.

Business rates. National living wage. Both rising from April.

And inside the existing arrangements:

Pharmacy First pays £17 per consultation.
Up to your cap.
Your cap is based on what you actually delivered last year.
Above it? Nothing.

So the pharmacies that grew their Pharmacy First delivery the hardest are now capped against that performance.

And in February 2026, drug price concessions hit a three-year high.
174 products pharmacies could not source at Drug Tariff price.
CPE called it a concerning escalation.
One in four pharmacy teams spending more than two hours a day just sourcing alternatives.

This is not one bad thing.
It is several bad things operating simultaneously.

The pharmacies absorbing this without falling back are not doing it by hoping the contract improves.

They are getting more clinical output from the same team.
They are reducing how much of the pharmacist’s day goes into routine checking.
They are building the services revenue line that has no cap.

💬 How is your pharmacy managing the pressure right now? One word or a number below.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyFirst #CPCF #PharmacyFunding #NHSPharmacy #PharmacyBusiness

posts.idea: Industry stance / Tier 1 carousel. Angle: 'The 3 workflows that break first when you grow.' Opens with a named observation (pharmacists adding staff instead of fixing systems), then walks through the 3 choke points (handoffs, repeats, phone-driven admin) and what scale without chaos actually requires. Function: Positioning. Hook candidate: 'Adding staff without upgrading systems is just multiplying the chaos.'
posts.post_caption:
Adding staff to a broken system does not fix it.

It just means more people absorbing the same chaos.

Most pharmacies that struggle to scale hit the same three choke points:

1. Handoffs. When a prescription moves between team members, the process depends on whoever is holding it. Not on a system. On a person.

2. Repeats. MDS, Nomad, regular patients. Managed manually, they eat the morning before the real work starts.

3. Phone-driven admin. Prescriptions chased by phone. Patients called by phone. Queries resolved by phone. The bench runs on calls it should never have to make.

None of these get better with more staff.
They get worse, just more expensively.

The pharmacies that actually scale without chaos fix the flow first.
Then grow the team into a system that can carry it.

👇 Which of these three kills your morning first?

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyWorkflow #PharmacyBusiness #Dispensing

posts.idea:
CUSTOMER TRANSFORMATION STORY — Formula slot B2 (THE anchor post).

Geoff Thomas, Malpas Pharmacy + Malpas Brook Pharmacy, Newport, Wales.
Two branches, 0.6 miles apart. Combined 40,000 items/month.
Grew from 2,500 items when he opened.
Six ACPs between both sites. Three consultation rooms, two more opening after Easter.
Early Titan adopter — started via nursing home tech (Investec), trialled at new branch Dec 2019, main branch 2020.

Architecture: Finish/Start/Finish — open with the scale, go back to the pressure, show what changed, callback to the numbers.

6-slide carousel:
Slide 1 — Hook: "2,500 items. Now 40,000." Bold numbers. Dark background.
Slide 2 — Before: "The pharmacist was the funnel. Everything came at you." The pressure of final checking everything.
Slide 3 — The breaking point: working after hours, worrying at home, weekends lost.
Slide 4 — The shift: clinical checking upfront, release prescriptions, trust the process.
Slide 5 — The proof: errors a fraction, weekends back, 6 ACPs, 3 consultation rooms (2 more coming).
Slide 6 — Close: callback to 40,000 items. "And the pharmacist isn't trapped in the dispensary."

Design: Titan PMR brand. Dark backgrounds with bold white numbers. Photo of Geoff if available. Quote highlight on "They wouldn't feel safe on another system."
posts.post_caption:
2,500 items a month when Geoff opened Malpas Pharmacy.

Now he runs two branches. 40,000 items between them.

Before Titan, the pharmacist was the funnel. Everything came at you. Final check on every item. Working after you locked the doors just to catch up.

He used to go home worrying. Turning it over in his head. Did I check that right?

Clinical checking upfront changed everything.

Release the prescriptions. Trust the process. The team handles the rest.

✅ Dispensing errors down to a fraction
✅ Weekends back
✅ Six ACPs across both branches
✅ Three consultation rooms open, two more coming

His staff won't go back. They wouldn't feel safe on another system.

Geoff didn't just grow his volume. He built the infrastructure to actually use it.

40,000 items a month. And the pharmacist isn't trapped in the dispensary.

💬 What would your pharmacy look like if the volume didn't own you?

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyWorkflow #DigitalPharmacy #PharmacyFirst #AIinPharmacy

Dervis
Pharmacy Owner
(Name bold, role in #8b949e)

titanverse.co.uk bottom centre in muted text.

posts.post_caption:
When you think of a camera brand, what brand do you think of?

Yeah, we were confused at first.

That's the question Dervis asked us. At first, we had no idea where he was going with it.

Then he landed it:

"If they have a healthcare concern, I want them to think Healthy-U. I don't want them thinking any other provider out there."

That's the bar.

Not just being known.
Being the name people think of first when they want better care, better outcomes, or a better quality of life.

And that does not come from surface-level marketing.

It comes from how the pharmacy actually behaves.

📊 Using patient data to show up proactively
📩 Communicating at the right time
🔁 Following up properly
❤️ Delivering services that genuinely help people move forward

So when a patient thinks about Healthy-U, they are not just thinking about a logo.

They are thinking:

- They know me.
- They look after me.
- They help me get better.

That's what Dervis is building.

And it's exactly why service infrastructure matters.

Because this kind of trust does not come from one-off transactions. It comes from consistent, connected care that people remember.

#Titanverse #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyServices #PatientExperience

posts.idea:
Carousel post. Titanverse brand. Pink-magenta to purple gradient (#FF65DD to #B752FF). 9 slides total.

Visual reference: Buff Motion 'Motion Insights' carousel. Alternating gradient and dark (near-black) slides. Single idea per slide. Large Anton typography. Generous white space. No clutter. Editorial layout system — wide safe margin (~10% canvas width), left-aligned throughout, four type styles only: utility label, Anton headline, DM Sans supporting line, metadata.

SLIDE 1 - COVER (gradient)
'Five questions every pharmacy owner should answer before they build a service model.'
Headline block starts ~20% from top, ~60% width, 5-6 stacked lines forming a clean tall rectangle.
Footnote bottom left, DM Sans small: 'Tariq Muhammad, Pharmacy Business, March 2026'
No photo. No name in headline. Titanverse wordmark top right, small.

SLIDE 2 - PREMISE (dark)
First block: 'Most owners pick the tools before they've worked out the plan.'
Gap of ~5-7% slide height.
Sting below: 'That's the wrong order.' — same or slightly larger size, lower opacity.
Two distinct text masses, not one block.

SLIDE 3 - Q1 (gradient)
Label top left: 'Q1'
'What services are you providing?'
'Private, NHS, or both?'

SLIDE 4 - Q2 (dark)
Label: 'Q2'
'How will you market to your patients?'
'How will you connect with them and keep them coming back?'

SLIDE 5 - Q3 (gradient)
Label: 'Q3'
'What is your commercial model?'
'How will you differentiate yourself from your competitors?'

SLIDE 6 - Q4 (dark) — starkest slide
Label: 'Q4'
'How much money are you likely to make?'
Nothing else. Largest type in the carousel. Single compact block floating in space.

SLIDE 7 - Q5 (gradient)
Label: 'Q5'
'What skills do you or your team need to develop?' (exact wording — 'you or your' is intentional)
'And what changes do you need to make to your dispensing operation?'

SLIDE 8 - LOGIC (dark)
Two blocks with a clear gap between them:
First: 'Only by answering these questions will you work out the value of moving into this new world — or whether it's all worth it.'
Second (smaller, lower opacity): 'Once you have a plan, only then can you work backwards to what you actually need to get there.'
The phrase 'or whether it's all worth it' must be kept — it is the honest challenge in Tariq's argument.

SLIDE 9 - REVEAL (gradient)
Photo right: Tariq's portrait from Pharmacy Business March 2026 article. Clean vertical rectangle, ~42-50% slide width, nearly full height. Right-weighted.
Text left, lower third:
Line 1 — Anton, large: 'Tariq Muhammad'
Line 2 — DM Sans, medium: 'CEO, Titan PMR & Titanverse'
Line 3 — DM Sans, smaller: 'Pharmacy Business, March 2026'
Titanverse wordmark bottom left. Nothing else.

posts.post_caption:
"We were wasting time chasing repeats before Titan. Now that time goes into services and revenue."

That's Rahul at Puri Pharmacy.

Here's what repeat management looks like without a system:

- Confirm a date with the patient
- Calculate when the order needs to go to the surgery
- File it away and remember to pull it in time
- Fax it or type out an email
- Wait for the prescription to come back
- Manually reconcile what the surgery sent
- Chase anything that's missing

Every patient. Every time.

Now after the first entry, Titan handles the whole thing automatically. Orders go out on time. Prescriptions get reconciled before the pharmacist sees them. Missing items get flagged without anyone remembering to check.

The team only touches it when there's a genuine clinical reason to.

"If your repeats rely on memory and chasing, you don't have a system. You have a bottleneck."

Same team. Different system.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyLife #RepeatPrescriptions

posts.idea:
A pain-led static post.


Angle: repeat prescriptions should not depend on one person, a spreadsheet, and pure memory.


This is the recognition post. It should speak to the pharmacies where repeats live in someone’s head, where people are chasing surgeries, checking spreadsheets, and hoping nothing gets missed.


A simple quote-style visual or clean graphic is enough. Don’t do a carousel. Don’t over-explain it.


A decent hook would be:


If your repeat system falls apart when one person is off, it isn’t a system.


That’s sharp, clear, and sets up Wednesday nicely.
posts.post_caption:
Some repeat systems have been seriously makeshift.

And you’ve got to give pharmacies credit.

A spreadsheet.
A reminders list.
An email chasing a missing item.
And then the repeat person goes on holiday.

And that’s just one workaround. Most pharmacies have several.

It feels archaic because it is.

Titan Repeat exists to replace that patchwork with a proper system.

Learn more: bit.ly/4seaaOD

#TitanPMR #TitanRepeat #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyWorkflow

Service growth did not start with adding more people.

That is what Titan changes.

Most people talk about dispensing errors like they’re a staffing problem.

More checks. More pressure. More people trying to be more careful.

But that misses the point.

Because when near misses keep happening, it usually is not because the team does not care.

It is because the system is asking human beings to hold too much together.

That was the reality for Asif on ProScript.

“We would have between 5 to 6 near misses a month easily on ProScript.”

Same pharmacy.
Same responsibility.
Different system.

“I’ve had 2 since we’ve had Titan… and that’s in a year. And they were human errors”

That is the shift.

The problem was never effort.
The problem was the workflow.

“Now I have 0 worries. Honestly it’s the best thing that’s happened.”

Good pharmacy software should not just save clicks.
It should make the pharmacy safer to run.

Titan does that.

posts.post_caption:
People still think of pharmacies as chemists.

But inside many of them, something very different is happening.

They are quietly becoming clinical diagnostic hubs.

Pharmacists are not just dispensing anymore.
They are running consultations.
Triaging conditions.
Delivering treatment.

Sounds like progress.

But here is the problem.

Most clinical services are still held together with five or six disconnected tools.

Booking platforms that do not talk to consultation software
Paper SOPs and policies
WhatsApp for governance
Spreadsheets for reporting

Too many systems.
Too much typing.
Nothing actually connected.

And this is the part nobody talks about.

The shift to clinical pharmacy is not the hard part.

Scaling it is.

What determines whether clinical pharmacy scales is not just demand, training or ambition.

It is the infrastructure behind it.

That is what Titanverse is built for.

Connecting the moving parts of clinical services so pharmacists can practise at the top of their licence, not buried in admin.

👉 Register your interest: https://bit.ly/4uqi3ly

posts.idea: CPCF 2026/27 contract reactive carousel. By Thursday = day 10 of the new financial year with no agreed contract. Fact-checked: cap based on trailing 3-month window (not 'last year'), 'No fee' above cap (not 'Nothing' — medicines still reimbursed). Clean runway on Thursday, no Titanverse post.
posts.post_caption:
The new financial year started on 1 April.

The 2026/27 pharmacy contract has not been agreed.

Negotiations are still ongoing. Current arrangements are rolling forward.

Which means pharmacies are operating under a settlement that was already inadequate last year, while costs have gone up again.

Business rates. National living wage. Both rising from April.

And inside the existing arrangements:

Pharmacy First pays £17 per consultation. Up to your cap. Your cap is based on what you actually delivered. Above it? No fee.

So the pharmacies that grew their Pharmacy First delivery the hardest are now capped against that performance.

And in February 2026, drug price concessions hit a three-year high. 174 products pharmacies could not source at Drug Tariff price. CPE called it a concerning escalation. One in four pharmacy teams spending more than two hours a day just sourcing alternatives.

This is not one bad thing. It is several bad things operating simultaneously.

The pharmacies absorbing this without falling back are not doing it by hoping the contract improves.

They are getting more clinical output from the same team. They are reducing how much of the pharmacist's day goes into routine checking. They are building the services revenue line that has no cap.

💬 How is your pharmacy managing the pressure right now? One word or a number below.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyFirst #CPCF #PharmacyFunding #NHSPharmacy #PharmacyBusiness

posts.idea:
Rahul Titanverse Transformation — Longform Video, 2-3 minutes

---
EDITOR REFERENCE — FULL STRUCTURE WITH TIMESTAMPS:

0:00–0:15 HOOK
Timestamp 9:09–9:15
"Honestly, I can't believe that AI transcription wasn't a thing in pharmacy before."
(Clean hook take, straight to camera. Rehearsed delivery.)

0:15–0:45 THE BEFORE — ProScript & the old world
Timestamp 1:53–2:56
"We got two pharmacies. We were using ProScript... ProScript is quite archaic. It requires you printing off a batch of prescriptions, loads of labels, but it just slowed your whole process down. Baskets were everywhere. Pharmacists had to be actively involved in checking, and it meant that pharmacists didn't have enough time to do the majority of their NHS services, or even start to think about private services."

Also weave in (6:13–7:07):
"Writing notes would have been on scraps of paper... you're either typing something, not looking at the patient, or you're scribbling things down... note taking was, in fairness, pretty poor."

0:45–1:30 THE SHIFT — Titanverse changes everything
Timestamp 9:15–9:38 (continuation of hook):
"As soon as we were aware that something had existed in a PMR system, it meant that we could create a safe system. Didn't have to think about using another software, copying and pasting or uploading it elsewhere. It was all in one software."

Timestamp 11:36–12:37:
"What we didn't realize with Titanverse was how everything was under one roof. We could send a message to a patient to confirm their appointment... questionnaires were built into it... it meant that we could create a far more streamlined service... Previously we might have thought we could only do five consultations in an hour, and now we can do more."

Timestamp 12:37–13:10:
"Titanverse is a game changer. Having something linked to your PMR means you don't have to have multiple tabs open. You have it all under one system. And the fact that it's web based, if I'm in one branch I can access the information for a patient at my other branch."

1:30–2:00 THE PROOF — How it works day to day
Timestamp 13:24–14:29:
Rahul walks through the actual consultation flow: pre-consult questionnaires sent via SMS, clinician reviews before appointment, AI transcription runs during consult, summary generated, full transcript stored, vitals recorded, prescription sent to Titan for dispensing.

Timestamp 19:45–20:15:
"Multiple hours a day... we've been able to probably double the amount of consultations that we're doing. The standard of our notes is completely improved."

Timestamp 21:13–21:50:
"We've seen that we can now offer twice as many consults, and that's kind of proof in what we're doing."

2:00–2:30 THE INDUSTRY VIEW — Why this matters now
Timestamp 24:02–24:52:
"Long gone are the days that pharmacies should be regarded as just dispensing outlets... funding is completely pulling the rug underneath pharmacies... now thinking about offering private services but high standard and high quality of care... we now use systems like Titan and Titanverse to deliver this in a digital age where patients' expectations are at the highest."

Timestamp 28:37–29:22:
"People still regard pharmacies as chemists. We're now clinical diagnostic hubs. We're supporting people through whether it be the NHS arm or the private arm."

2:30–2:45 THE CLOSE
Timestamp 37:47:
"Pharmacy will not survive if you just do NHS services in 2026."

End card: Titanverse logo + titanverse.co.uk

---
BONUS SECTIONS (use if extending or for B-roll narration):

DemDx integration (16:30–19:30): Rahul explains DemDx clinical pathways, pharmacy first PGDs, clinical calculators. Very detailed, good for an integration-specific cut.

Templates (21:55–22:40): Custom template building in Titanverse for common conditions and continuous care patients.

Pharmacy renovation (23:16–24:00): Renovated pharmacy specifically for services, two consultation rooms, wouldn't have done it without switching to Titan/Titanverse.

Scaling advice (39:39–43:30): "Being impatient and not investing in systems" — research your market, each private service is its own business, pharmacy undersells itself. Very quotable.

TitanUp tease (46:35–47:08): "If an opportunity like TitanUp came to connect everyone... to discuss the future of pharmacy, then of course we'll be there. It's now about collaborating and working with one another."

posts.post_caption:
They turned off stock control for one month.

Not because it wasn't working. Because they wanted to test if it actually made a difference.

The result?

What used to take 45 minutes started taking two hours every morning. 100 lines coming in instead of 10. Staff more anxious. Reconciliation chaos.

In Jeet's words: "absolute agony."

They turned it back on.

Sometimes the best proof isn't a demo.

It's what happens when you take it away.

#TitanPMR #StockControl #PharmacyEfficiency
posts.name: TITAN_AbsoluteAgony_Jeet
